threats.title: Unbounded TGT Deposits Cause TITN Insolvency: totalTitnClaimable Exceeds Contract Balance
threats.hypothesis_description: The onTokenTransfer function accepts unlimited TGT deposits without enforcing a cumulative cap against TGT_TO_EXCHANGE or verifying that totalTitnClaimable does not exceed the TITN balance. If total TGT deposited exceeds TGT_TO_EXCHANGE (579M TGT), the cumulative claimable TITN exceeds TITN_ARB (173.7M TITN), making the contract insolvent. TGT total supply is 1B; at full rate in the first 90 days, depositing all TGT would allocate ~300M TITN against only 173.7M available. Early claimers drain the pool; later users' claimTitn calls revert on safeTransfer. This also cascades into withdrawRemainingTitn underflow (OPS-H002).
threats.investigation_rationale: The quoteTitn function calculates per-deposit TITN output without checking cumulative allocation. No state variable tracks total TGT deposited or enforces totalTitnClaimable <= TITN_ARB. This is exploitable by any TGT holder through normal on-chain operations.

proof_of_signal.pattern: onTokenTransfer computes titnOut via quoteTitn and increments claimableTitnPerUser and totalTitnClaimable without any cumulative cap check against TITN_ARB or contract TITN balance

preconditions: Total TGT supply exceeds TGT_TO_EXCHANGE (579M TGT)
lockedStatus != Locked and launchTime set
Within 360-day window
TGT implements ERC677 transferAndCall calling onTokenTransfer

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: quoteTitn calculates per-deposit output but no accumulator verifies total allocation stays within TITN_ARB budget
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: No admin intervention needed; any TGT holder can trigger insolvency by depositing
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: Full 360-day merge window; most damaging in first 90 days at full conversion rate

operational_questions: What is the total circulating supply of TGT that could be deposited?
What percentage of TGT holders are likely to participate in the merge?
At what deposit volume does totalTitnClaimable exceed actual TITN balance?
validation_requirements: Verify TGT total supply and distribution
Simulate totalTitnClaimable growth vs TITN_ARB with varying deposit volumes
Confirm no off-chain mechanism prevents over-deposit

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Acquire TGT tokens on Arbitrum (market purchase or existing holdings)
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Deposit TGT via transferAndCall during first 90 days at full rate, pushing totalTitnClaimable above TITN_ARB
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Immediately call claimTitn to extract allocated TITN before other users
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Later depositors and claimers find safeTransfer reverts due to insufficient TITN balance
preliminary_attack_methodology.coordination_requirements: None - single attacker with sufficient TGT can trigger insolvency

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Complete insolvency of merge contract; late claimers permanently locked out of TITN
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Owner must deposit additional TITN to cover deficit; no on-chain recovery mechanism
potential_operational_impact.affected_operations: claimTitn and withdrawRemainingTitn for all users who haven't claimed

execution_requirements_analysis.governance_tokens_needed: TGT tokens exceeding TGT_TO_EXCHANGE collectively (579M+ TGT across all depositors)
execution_requirements_analysis.coordination_complexity: Low - natural market participation can trigger this without malicious intent
execution_requirements_analysis.operational_timing: Most effective in first 90 days at full conversion rate

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Monitor totalTitnClaimable approaching or exceeding TITN_ARB
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Large TGT deposits in rapid succession
investigation_priorities.behavioral_patterns: Immediate claimTitn calls after large deposits (bank-run behavior)

threats.title: withdrawRemainingTitn Arithmetic Underflow Permanently DoSes All Post-Year Claims
threats.hypothesis_description: In withdrawRemainingTitn, the expression `unclaimedTitn = remainingTitnAfter1Year - initialTotalClaimable` underflows (reverts in Solidity 0.8+) when the contract's TITN balance at the 360-day snapshot is less than totalTitnClaimable. This occurs when TGT deposits exceed TGT_TO_EXCHANGE (OPS-H001) or if the owner has withdrawn TITN. Since remainingTitnAfter1Year is initialized by the first caller and re-initialized on every call when it equals 0, if the balance is 0 the function permanently reverts for all users.
threats.investigation_rationale: The subtraction has no underflow guard and depends on the invariant that contract balance >= totalTitnClaimable, which is not enforced anywhere in the code.

proof_of_signal.pattern: unclaimedTitn = remainingTitnAfter1Year - initialTotalClaimable performs unchecked subtraction; if remainingTitnAfter1Year < initialTotalClaimable, Solidity 0.8 reverts with arithmetic underflow

preconditions: 360 days have elapsed since launchTime
Contract TITN balance < totalTitnClaimable at time of first withdrawRemainingTitn call
At least one user has claimableTitnPerUser > 0

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: First withdrawRemainingTitn caller snapshots balance that may be below totalTitnClaimable, causing all subsequent calls to revert
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: Can occur without admin action if TGT over-deposit (OPS-H001) occurs; also triggered by owner withdraw
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: Permanent after 360 days if insolvency conditions exist

operational_questions: Under what realistic scenarios does contract balance fall below totalTitnClaimable?
Is there any fallback mechanism to recover from this permanent DoS?
Can the owner deposit additional TITN after 360 days to fix the underflow?
validation_requirements: Simulate withdrawRemainingTitn with balance < totalTitnClaimable
Verify that the function permanently reverts once remainingTitnAfter1Year is set to 0
Test edge case: balance == 0 causing perpetual re-initialization loop

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Trigger insolvency via OPS-H001 (over-deposit of TGT) during the 360-day window
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Claim own TITN via claimTitn before 360 days
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: After 360 days, all remaining users cannot call withdrawRemainingTitn due to underflow
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Remaining TITN in contract (if any) is permanently locked

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: All post-year claims permanently DoSed; users cannot retrieve their allocated TITN
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Owner may attempt to deposit TITN to increase balance above totalTitnClaimable before first call, but if first call already failed with balance=0, remainingTitnAfter1Year stays 0 and re-initializes each time
potential_operational_impact.affected_operations: withdrawRemainingTitn for all users with unclaimed TITN

execution_requirements_analysis.governance_tokens_needed: None directly; insolvency is the precondition
execution_requirements_analysis.technical_skills: Basic - just call withdrawRemainingTitn after 360 days

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Compare titn.balanceOf(MergeTgt) with totalTitnClaimable after 360 days

threats.title: Cross-Chain Dust Bridging Permanently Restricts Arbitrary Addresses on Base
threats.hypothesis_description: The _credit function in Titn.sol permanently sets isBridgedTokenHolder[_to] = true for any address receiving bridged tokens. An attacker can bridge a dust amount (e.g., 1 wei) of TITN from Arbitrum to any victim address on Base via LayerZero OFT. Once flagged, the victim cannot transfer their TITN on Base (when isBridgedTokensTransferLocked is true) except to the owner, transferAllowedContract, or lzEndpoint. No function exists to clear this flag. The attack cost is only the LayerZero bridging fee (~$0.10-$1).
threats.investigation_rationale: isBridgedTokenHolder is a one-way flag set in _credit with no clearing mechanism. _validateTransfer enforces restrictions based on this flag. On Base (chainId != 42161), only flagged addresses are restricted, making the griefing attack targeted.

proof_of_signal.pattern: _credit unconditionally sets isBridgedTokenHolder[_to] = true for any recipient; no minimum amount check; no mechanism to clear flag

preconditions: Attacker holds any amount of TITN on a source chain
isBridgedTokensTransferLocked is true on Base
LayerZero cross-chain messaging is operational
Victim has TITN holdings on Base or will acquire TITN on Base in the future

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: _credit marks recipient without any validation of amount or recipient consent; flag is permanent with no admin override
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: None - attacker acts independently; admin cannot clear per-address flags
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: Permanent - as long as cross-chain bridging is operational

operational_questions: Can the owner mitigate this by setting isBridgedTokensTransferLocked = false?
Is there a practical cost threshold that makes this attack uneconomical at scale?
What downstream protocols on Base would be affected by users unable to transfer TITN?
validation_requirements: Test bridging 1 wei to arbitrary address on Base and confirm flag is set
Verify no function exists to clear isBridgedTokenHolder
Confirm _validateTransfer blocks flagged addresses from all transfers except exempted destinations

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Acquire minimal TITN on Arbitrum (via TGT merge or market purchase)
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Call OFT.send() with victim's address as recipient, bridging dust amount to Base
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: LayerZero delivers message; _credit on Base mints dust to victim and sets isBridgedTokenHolder[victim] = true
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Victim's TITN transfers on Base permanently revert unless to/from transferAllowedContract or owner

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Targeted users permanently unable to trade/transfer TITN on Base; could target DEX LPs, stakers, or key holders
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Only global mitigation: owner sets isBridgedTokensTransferLocked = false, removing restrictions for ALL bridged holders
potential_operational_impact.affected_operations: All transfer and transferFrom calls by flagged addresses on Base

execution_requirements_analysis.coordination_complexity: Very low - automated script can flag thousands of addresses
execution_requirements_analysis.operational_timing: Any time while cross-chain bridging is live

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Small cross-chain transfers to many distinct addresses from single source
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Addresses flagged as bridged holders who never initiated bridging

threats.title: Off-By-One at Exactly 360 Days: TGT Deposited for Zero TITN Output
threats.hypothesis_description: At exactly block.timestamp - launchTime == 360 days, onTokenTransfer allows a deposit (since the check `> 360 days` is false), but quoteTitn returns 0 (since timeSinceLaunch is not `< 90 days` and not `< 360 days`, falling to the else branch). The user's TGT is irrevocably transferred into the contract while claimableTitnPerUser gains 0. Furthermore, claimTitn immediately reverts (block.timestamp - launchTime >= 360 days), and withdrawRemainingTitn requires claimableTitn > 0 which is also 0. The user permanently loses their TGT with no recovery path.
threats.investigation_rationale: Boundary inconsistency between onTokenTransfer's > check and quoteTitn's < check at exactly 360 days. No minimum output validation exists in onTokenTransfer.

proof_of_signal.pattern: onTokenTransfer uses `block.timestamp - launchTime > 360 days` (strict greater-than) while quoteTitn uses `timeSinceLaunch < 360 days` (strict less-than); at exactly 360 days neither condition catches the edge case

preconditions: block.timestamp - launchTime == 360 days exactly
lockedStatus != Locked
User calls TGT.transferAndCall with amount > 0

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Deposit accepted with zero output; no minimum output check exists in onTokenTransfer

operational_questions: How likely is a natural deposit at the exact 360-day block timestamp?
Could a user be guided to deposit at this exact time by a malicious frontend?
Is there any mechanism to recover TGT from the contract for zero-output deposits?
validation_requirements: Simulate onTokenTransfer at block.timestamp - launchTime == 360 days
Confirm quoteTitn returns 0 at this boundary
Verify no claim path exists for 0-TITN deposits

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Deposit TGT via transferAndCall - deposit succeeds but titnOut = 0
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: User's TGT is locked in contract with 0 claimable TITN
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: No claim function accepts the user's call (claimTitn reverts, withdrawRemainingTitn requires claimableTitn > 0)

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Permanent loss of deposited TGT for affected users
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Only owner withdraw can return TGT; no automated recovery path
potential_operational_impact.affected_operations: Any user depositing at the exact 360-day boundary

threats.title: lzEndpoint Transfer Exemption Is Dead Code That Creates Permanent Token Loss Vector
threats.hypothesis_description: The _validateTransfer function exempts transfers where `to == lzEndpoint`, ostensibly to allow bridging. However, OFT v2's send() function uses internal _burn which bypasses the transfer/transferFrom overrides entirely. The exemption serves no purpose for actual bridging. Instead, it creates a dangerous token loss vector: on Arbitrum where all other transfers are restricted, users may attempt to bridge by calling transfer(lzEndpoint, amount) directly. This sends tokens to the endpoint contract address where they are permanently stuck - no bridging occurs and no recovery mechanism exists.
threats.investigation_rationale: The protocol documentation states users on Arbitrum should transfer to the LayerZero endpoint. But the actual OFT bridging mechanism uses _burn, making the transfer exemption misleading dead code that enables permanent token loss.

proof_of_signal.pattern: _validateTransfer exempts `to != lzEndpoint` — allows ERC20 transfer to endpoint address which does NOT trigger cross-chain bridging; OFT._debit() uses _burn() which completely bypasses transfer() override

preconditions: User is on Arbitrum (all transfers restricted)
User calls transfer(lzEndpoint, amount) instead of OFT.send()
Tokens transferred to endpoint address have no recovery mechanism

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Transfer to lzEndpoint succeeds but tokens are not bridged; they are stuck at the endpoint address
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: Any time while contract is active on Arbitrum

operational_questions: Can any tokens sent to the lzEndpoint address be recovered?
Does the LayerZero endpoint contract have a sweep function?
How many users might attempt direct transfer to lzEndpoint instead of OFT.send()?
validation_requirements: Confirm OFT._debit uses _burn (bypasses transfer override)
Verify transfer(lzEndpoint, amount) succeeds on Arbitrum but does not bridge
Check if lzEndpoint contract has any token recovery mechanism

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: User on Arbitrum attempts to bridge TITN by calling transfer(lzEndpoint, amount)
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Tokens now sit at the endpoint contract address - no cross-chain message is generated
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Tokens are permanently lost with no recovery path
preliminary_attack_methodology.coordination_requirements: None - user error facilitated by misleading code

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Permanent token loss for affected users
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Likely unrecoverable - depends on LayerZero endpoint contract
potential_operational_impact.affected_operations: Any user who calls transfer to lzEndpoint directly

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: ERC20 Transfer events to lzEndpoint address
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: TITN accumulating at the endpoint address
investigation_priorities.behavioral_patterns: Users transferring to lzEndpoint without corresponding bridge events

threats.title: Irreversible isBridgedTokenHolder Flag Prevents Granular Admin Remediation
threats.hypothesis_description: The isBridgedTokenHolder mapping can only transition from false to true (in _credit) and has no setter function for the owner to clear it per-address. If the owner temporarily sets isBridgedTokensTransferLocked = false to allow transfers and then re-enables it, ALL previously flagged addresses (including legitimately unflagged ones from OPS-H003 griefing) are restricted again. The only admin remediation is the global toggle, which removes restrictions for all bridged holders simultaneously - there is no granular per-address override.
threats.investigation_rationale: Missing admin function to clear isBridgedTokenHolder for specific addresses. Combined with the griefing vector (OPS-H003), this means the owner cannot selectively remediate affected addresses without removing all transfer restrictions globally.

proof_of_signal.pattern: isBridgedTokenHolder[_to] set to true in _credit; no function exists in the contract to set it back to false; owner has no per-address override capability

preconditions: At least one address has been griefed via OPS-H003 or legitimately unflagged
Owner wishes to selectively allow transfers for specific addresses
isBridgedTokensTransferLocked is true

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: No setter for isBridgedTokenHolder exists in the contract; Ownable inheritance does not provide mapping overrides
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: Admin WANTS to help but lacks the tool to do so per-address
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: Permanent - structural limitation of the contract

operational_questions: Is there a planned mechanism to clear bridged holder flags?
Would a proxy upgrade be needed to add this capability?
What is the operational impact of only having a global toggle?
validation_requirements: Confirm no function in Titn.sol or inherited contracts can modify isBridgedTokenHolder
Verify that the contract is not upgradeable

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Attacker griefs multiple addresses via OPS-H003
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Admin can only set isBridgedTokensTransferLocked = false globally (removing ALL restrictions)
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: If admin re-enables lock, all griefed addresses are restricted again
preliminary_attack_methodology.coordination_requirements: None for the attacker; admin is structurally unable to remediate

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Admin forced into all-or-nothing transfer restriction management
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Requires contract upgrade or migration to fix structurally
potential_operational_impact.affected_operations: All transfer operations for flagged addresses on non-Arbitrum chains

threats.title: TwoWay LockedStatus Enum Defined But Functionally Identical to OneWay - Missing Reverse Functionality
threats.hypothesis_description: The IMerge interface defines three LockedStatus values: Locked, OneWay, and TwoWay. However, the onTokenTransfer function only checks `lockedStatus == LockedStatus.Locked`, treating OneWay and TwoWay identically. No code path exists that differentiates TwoWay from OneWay. If TwoWay was intended to enable reversible deposits (returning TGT to depositors in exchange for TITN), this functionality is completely missing. Users or governance processes expecting a TwoWay mode to provide deposit reversal have no recourse.
threats.investigation_rationale: The enum explicitly names a TwoWay state suggesting bidirectional exchange was planned. The absence of any differentiating code means this is either dead code or missing functionality.

proof_of_signal.pattern: onTokenTransfer only checks `lockedStatus == LockedStatus.Locked`; no code anywhere differentiates TwoWay from OneWay; no reverse exchange function exists

preconditions: Owner sets lockedStatus to TwoWay
Users expect TwoWay to enable deposit reversal

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: TwoWay enum value exists but has zero functional differentiation from OneWay
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: Owner can set TwoWay but it has no unique effect

operational_questions: Was TwoWay intended to enable reverse exchanges (TITN → TGT)?
Are users or documentation referencing TwoWay functionality?
Is there a planned upgrade to implement TwoWay?
validation_requirements: Confirm no code path differentiates TwoWay from OneWay
Search for any reverse exchange function in the contract

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Users deposit TGT expecting TwoWay mode to allow reversal
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: No reversal function exists - TGT is permanently locked
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Users are misled by the TwoWay status into irrevocable deposits
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Only owner withdraw() can return TGT

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Users may irreversibly deposit TGT under false TwoWay expectations
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Only owner can return TGT via withdraw()

threats.title: Owner Can Drain All TITN From MergeTgt Via Unrestricted withdraw() Including User-Allocated Amounts
threats.hypothesis_description: The withdraw() function allows the owner to transfer any amount of any ERC20 token from the MergeTgt contract, including the TITN allocated to users via claimableTitnPerUser. There is no check that the withdrawal preserves solvency (titn.balanceOf >= totalTitnClaimable). The owner can drain all TITN, permanently blocking all claimTitn and withdrawRemainingTitn calls which rely on safeTransfer that would revert with insufficient balance. This also corrupts the withdrawRemainingTitn snapshot if called before the 360-day mark.
threats.investigation_rationale: The withdraw function has no restrictions on token type or amount. It accepts TITN as the token parameter and can transfer user-allocated funds to the owner. The solvency invariant (INV-ECON-001) is directly violated.

proof_of_signal.pattern: withdraw(IERC20 token, uint256 amount) performs safeTransfer to owner() with no validation that token != titn or that amount preserves solvency

preconditions: Owner account is compromised or acts maliciously
MergeTgt holds TITN balance > 0
Users have claimableTitnPerUser > 0

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: withdraw accepts titn as token parameter with no solvency check; same function used for legitimate TGT recovery and malicious TITN extraction
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: Requires owner action - this IS the admin rug pull vector

operational_questions: Is the owner a multisig or EOA?
Are there any off-chain safeguards monitoring withdraw calls?
Is ownership transferred to a governance contract post-deployment?
validation_requirements: Verify withdraw(titn, titn.balanceOf(MergeTgt)) succeeds
Confirm subsequent claimTitn calls revert

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Owner (or compromised owner key) calls withdraw(titn, titn.balanceOf(address(this)))
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: All TITN transferred to owner address
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: All user claimTitn and withdrawRemainingTitn calls revert due to insufficient balance

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Withdraw events for TITN token from MergeTgt
investigation_priorities.behavioral_patterns: Owner withdraw followed by user claim reverts

threats.title: Owner Changing transferAllowedContract Permanently Blocks MergeTgt TITN Distributions
threats.hypothesis_description: On Arbitrum, all TITN transfers are restricted by the chain ID check in _validateTransfer. MergeTgt distributes TITN to users via safeTransfer (which calls transfer()). This only works because MergeTgt is set as transferAllowedContract in the deployment script. If the Titn owner calls setTransferAllowedContract(otherAddress), MergeTgt's transfers would be blocked by _validateTransfer (since from == MergeTgt is no longer exempted and block.chainid == 42161). All claimTitn and withdrawRemainingTitn calls would permanently revert.
threats.investigation_rationale: The deployment script sets transferAllowedContract to MergeTgt. But this can be changed at any time by the Titn owner. There's no immutability guarantee, and Titn owner and MergeTgt owner may be the same or different entities.

proof_of_signal.pattern: setTransferAllowedContract can change the single exempted address at any time; _validateTransfer on Arbitrum blocks all transfers not from/to this address; MergeTgt's safeTransfer(user) calls would fail if MergeTgt is no longer the exempted address

preconditions: Deployment on Arbitrum (chainId 42161)
Titn owner changes transferAllowedContract to any address other than MergeTgt
MergeTgt holds TITN and users have claimable balances

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Single transferAllowedContract means only one contract can be exempted at a time; changing it breaks existing integrations
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: Any time after transferAllowedContract is changed away from MergeTgt

operational_questions: Are Titn owner and MergeTgt owner the same entity?
Is there a planned change of transferAllowedContract?
Could this happen accidentally during operational updates?
validation_requirements: Verify MergeTgt claimTitn reverts after transferAllowedContract is changed
Confirm only one address can be exempted at a time

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: MergeTgt is no longer the exempted contract
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: All MergeTgt → user TITN transfers revert on Arbitrum
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: All user claims permanently blocked until transferAllowedContract is restored

potential_operational_impact.administrative_compromise: No compromise needed - operational error suffices
potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: All MergeTgt claims blocked until transferAllowedContract restored
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Recoverable by setting transferAllowedContract back to MergeTgt

investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: User claim reverts after transferAllowedContract change

threats.title: Owner Pre-Snapshot TITN Withdrawal Corrupts withdrawRemainingTitn Proportional Distribution
threats.hypothesis_description: The withdrawRemainingTitn function takes a one-time snapshot of the contract's TITN balance (remainingTitnAfter1Year) on the first post-360-day call. If the owner calls withdraw(titn, amount) before this snapshot, the snapshotted balance is reduced. The proportional bonus calculation uses this reduced snapshot: unclaimedTitn = remainingTitnAfter1Year - initialTotalClaimable. If the owner extracts only the surplus (TITN_ARB - totalTitnClaimable), unclaimedTitn becomes 0 and no user receives a bonus. If the owner extracts more, it causes the arithmetic underflow in OPS-H002.
threats.investigation_rationale: The snapshot mechanism trusts that the TITN balance at the 360-day mark accurately reflects the pool available for distribution. Owner withdrawal before the snapshot silently reduces all users' proportional bonus.

proof_of_signal.pattern: remainingTitnAfter1Year = titn.balanceOf(address(this)) on first call; no protection against owner having already reduced this balance via withdraw()

preconditions: Owner calls withdraw(titn, amount) before the 360-day mark
withdrawRemainingTitn has not yet been called (remainingTitnAfter1Year == 0)
Users have unclaimed TITN balances

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Owner withdrawal of TITN before snapshot reduces proportional bonus for all users; no event or guard prevents this
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: Between deposit and first withdrawRemainingTitn call (up to 360 days)

operational_questions: Does the owner have legitimate reasons to withdraw TITN before 360 days?
Is there monitoring for owner withdrawal of TITN from MergeTgt?
What is the expected surplus TITN amount?
validation_requirements: Calculate expected remainingTitnAfter1Year under normal conditions vs after owner withdrawal
Simulate proportional distribution with reduced snapshot

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Owner calls withdraw(titn, surplus) extracting excess TITN before 360 days
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: After 360 days, first user calls withdrawRemainingTitn
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: remainingTitnAfter1Year is snapshotted at reduced value
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: All users receive reduced or zero proportional bonus

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Users receive less than expected proportional bonus; complete DoS if underflow occurs
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Snapshot is permanent once set; cannot be re-initialized

execution_requirements_analysis.operational_timing: Before 360-day mark and before first withdrawRemainingTitn call

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Withdraw events for TITN from MergeTgt before 360-day mark
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: TITN balance decreasing without corresponding user claims
